definition: Being an active member of a political community, entitling a person to civil and political rights and implying corresponding civic duties.
The legal and state connection that binds a physical person to a sovereign State from birth onwards.
Inviolable and legally-protected prerogatives belonging to individuals to guarantee their liberty and human dignity.
The set of civic and moral obligations required by law to maintain the general interest and peaceful cohabitation.
The moral and legal obligation of accepting and owning the consequences of one's own decisions and actions.
Universal Declaration of Human Rights, a milestone document adopted in 1948 proclaiming inalienable rights for every individual.
United Nations, an international peace assembly created in 1945 to promote worldwide cooperation and human rights.
The historic year the UDHR was adopted in Paris by the United Nations General Assembly (December 10).
The legal principle that any accused person is considered innocent until proven guilty in a fair, legal trial.
The illegal or abusive use of a public office or influence to obtain an unfair personal or financial benefit.
A form of misuse of authority where people favor family members for jobs or positions, bypass merit.
An unfair practice of giving special privileges or advantages to friends or political links over other qualified people.
The unfair treatment of an individual because of their race, origin, religion, sex, age, or disability.
The social and cultural roles, identities, and behaviors that society attributes to males and females respectively.
Any intentional use of physical force against someone, resulting in pain, injury, or bodily harm.
Harassment, constant humiliation, or verbal outbursts designed to control, intimidate, and destroy a person's self-esteem.
An economic and social pattern that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet theirs.
One of the three sustainability pillars aiming to preserve nature, clean air, water tables, forests, and stable climates.
A moral and social duty of reciprocal aid that drives members of a community to support each other during hard times.
The practical engagement of individuals in public debates and the co-decision of local or national affairs.
Money or items of value offered to public officers to influence decisions, bypass regulations, or avoid a ticket.
Obtaining money, services, or consent from a person by using threats, force, or intimidation.
The illegal theft of public money or assets by the public officials in charge of managing or guarding them.
The principle that all human beings are born equal and deserve the same opportunities, rights, and legal protection.
The power or right to act, think, and express oneself autonomously, as long as it does not infringe on other people's safety and rights.
The set of binding rules made by parliament to maintain peace, safety, and proper relationships inside a society.
Mandatory financial contributions paid to the State to build shared infrastructures and safeguard social services.
The universal right to access schools and knowledge, enabling the intellectual growth and freedom of all individuals.
The collective ecosystems (water, air, soil, plants, wildlife) upon which human survival directly depends.
Sharing your time, work, or skills for free to support public interest causes or community development.
